title: The Macro Minute: Will DJT anchor on soaring stocks or gas prices in China and Iran talks this week?

Happy Monday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our macro minute for Monday, May
11th, 2026. Hope everyone had a
wonderful Mother's Day out there. Happy
Mother's Day to all the moms out there
on team 42. So, as always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
will President Trump anchor on soaring
stock prices or soaring gas prices in
negotiations with China and Iran this
week?
Our answer is President Trump and
Chinese President Xi Jinping are set to
meet on Thursday and Friday of this week
to discuss global energy supplies,
tariffs, and rare earth exports that
continue to pressure global supply
chains. POTUS is also expected to
challenge Xi on China's posture toward
Iran during the talks. This comes amid
the US and Iran rejecting each other's
latest peace proposals tied to ending
the 10-week conflict, underscoring how
fragile the current ceasefire remains
with both sides still far apart on key
issues.
Saudi Aramco warned that the global oil
market would require several months to
normalize, even if the straits are
removed or reopened immediately. As for
what it's worth, current betting odds
have the Republican Party comfortably
losing the House and at risk of losing
the Senate. So, the clock is ticking for
POTUS to determine whether he wants a
full military victory or a diplomatic
resolution to the conflict in the Middle
East.
Definitely won't get both. So, uh we'll
wrap it up there. And as always, we'll
transition to our uh 42 macro dashboard
and wrap up with a question from our
community. Uh this one is titled yield
curve control and credit spreads. Says,
"Hey, skipper. Uh so, if when we see
yield curve control implementation,
they're only managing US Treasuries uh
while the credit market remains in the
hands of investors. Uh would that
outcome not likely be widening spreads?
Uh usually that's not so great for
equities. Uh how do you see that uh
playing out?" Uh so, the first thing
I'll say is when you think about credit
spreads, you want to kind of decompose
what the drivers of credit spreads are.
Uh principally, you're going to be
worried about the business cycle. Where
are we in the business cycle? Was the
business cycle improving? Is it eroding?
Or are we heading to a downturn or
coming out of a downturn? That's your
that you think about the levels and
momentum of credit spreads, that's going
to be a PC1. I would say PC2 would be
the technicals in the fixed income
market, whether it be technicals are for
key issuers in the credit market, key
industries in the credit market.
Obviously, you think about the
hyperscalers and all the debt they've,
you know, kind of raised in recent
months as being a key driver now.
As well as the technicals in the broader
fixed income market in terms of the
supply demand of various segments of the
credit market. And so, when you kind of
put those two things together in a yield
curve control scenario, what's likely to
happen is that you're going to depress
the natural interest rate in the
economy. You're going to depress the the
sort of realized interest rate across
the economy to a level that's probably
below what the technicals in the fixed
income market of
sort of imply, which would ultimately
result in
a kind of a stimulative tailwind for the
broader business cycle. So, it's likely
that yield curve control would
ultimately perpetuate the business
cycle, not truncated in a way that would
cause a durable widening of credit
spreads. So, that's positive at the
margins. And secondarily, if you're
talking about yield curve control sort
of kind of removing duration risk from
global fixed income, ultimately you're
going to,
you know, sort of, you know, again,
keeping that interest rate depressed
relative to what a neutral level for the
economy was suggested should be, you're
ultimately going to have more demand for
the credit asset class because you're
going to have investors reaching for
yield because they're not getting the
yield that, you know, the ex-ante return
that they need from Treasury securities
to support that asset class. So, they're
going to increasingly, in our opinion,
look to the credit markets. There may be
borrowers in the credit market that
trade through Treasuries at some point
in the next 5 to 7 years, particularly
if we saw a material deterioration in
the US dollar's status as a global
reserve currency in the context of all
the dynamics the geopolitical dynamics
that we've highlighted in our investing
in a four turn regime analysis. So, I'll
wrap it up there. Darius Dale here
presenting our macro minute for Monday
May 11th, 2025 2026. Everyone have a
wonderful day out there. Best of luck.
We'll catch back here tomorrow. Cheers.
